identity.canonical_title: Example (Dipole Near Field) — Quick Symbolic Sketch: β

theory.canonical_statement: # 6) Example (dipole near field) — quick symbolic sketch Given your dipole vector potential (in the file) \[ A_{\rm dipole}(\mathbf r)=\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\rm eff}\times(\mathbf r-\mathbf r_{\rm cm})}{|\mathbf r-\mathbf r_{\rm cm}|^3}, \] compute \[ B=\nabla\times A_{\rm dipole},\qquad E=-\partial_t A - \nabla\Phi. \] Then build \[ T^{00}_{\rm EM} \simeq \mathcal{E}_{\rm EM},\qquad T^{0i}_{\rm EM}\simeq \frac{1}{c}S^i_{\rm EM},\qquad T^{ij}_{\rm EM}=\sigma^{ij}. \] If the dipole is slowly time-varying and the rotation is nonrelativistic (\(|\beta|\ll1\)), you can use these 3-vector forms directly and then correct order-by-order in \(\beta=(\omega\times r)/c\) per your Lorentz transform for the rotating frame. Your file already includes the Lorentz transformation pieces (the \(\Lambda\) matrix and \(\beta\), \(\gamma\)). fileciteturn0file0
theory.technical_interpretation: Example (Dipole Near Field) — Quick Symbolic Sketch: β is an algebraic definition, written β=(ω× r)/c. It is an algebraic definition expressing the quantity directly in terms of the variables on its right-hand side. It is built from ω, r, c, defining β. Within the Trawin Zero Point registry it serves as one element of the framework's mathematical narrative.
